facts_disclosed: Works at Lurie Children's Hospital, night shift

turns.content: hey so I really need to figure out how to stop feeling like a zombie because the night shifts at Lurie Children's are actually destroying me lol

Hey! Night shifts are brutal, especially in a pediatric hospital where the emotional weight is already heavy. How long have you been on the night shift schedule? And are you having trouble sleeping during the day, or is it more about the general exhaustion that builds up over time?

ok so I've been on nights for about 8 months now and honestly both?? like I get home at 7:30am and the sun is blasting through my windows and my brain is still wired from the shift and then I maybe fall asleep by 9 or 10 and then I'm up by 2pm and it's just not enough

That's a really common pattern with night shift workers — your body is fighting against daylight and its own circadian rhythm. A few things that can genuinely help: blackout curtains if you don't have them already, a consistent wind-down routine when you get home (even 20 minutes of something calm), and keeping your sleep schedule the same even on days off if possible. Have you tried any of those, or is your setup at home not great for daytime sleep?

I have these cheap curtains from target that kind of block light but not really lol my apartment in Logan Square gets SO much morning sun which is great except when you're trying to sleep at 8am after being up all night with a 4 year old who just had surgery

Oh yeah, Logan Square gets a lot of east-facing light in those older apartment buildings. Investing in actual blackout curtains or even the temporary blackout film you can stick on windows would be a game changer — it's like $15 on Amazon and makes your room cave-dark. And honestly, after a shift like that with a post-op kid, your nervous system is probably still in high alert mode. Do you do anything to decompress between getting home and trying to sleep?

honestly no I just kind of eat whatever is in the fridge and scroll my phone for a while which I KNOW is bad but my brain needs to turn off and that's how I do it. sometimes I call my mom but she's usually busy in the morning

The phone scrolling is so understandable — your brain wants to switch off, but the blue light and stimulation are actually keeping it revved up. What if you swapped even half of that scroll time for something like a podcast or audiobook with the lights dimmed? Same kind of "brain off" feeling but way less stimulating for your eyes. And eating is good — you need fuel after a shift — but maybe prep something warm and easy beforehand so you're not standing in front of the fridge making decisions at 8am when your brain is fried.

that's actually smart about the podcast thing I could do that. the meal prep is hard though because on my days off I literally do not want to think about being responsible for anything lol I spend all night being responsible for tiny humans

Ha, that's so valid. Being "on" all night for kids who need you is a completely different level of mental drain than a desk job. What about something that requires zero thought — like buying a bunch of frozen burritos or soup you can just microwave? No prep, no decisions, just heat and eat. The goal isn't gourmet, it's just making sure you have something warm that doesn't require brainpower. Save your energy for the things that actually matter.

frozen burritos are already like 40% of my diet so we're on the right track lmao. but real talk though the worst part isn't even the tiredness it's like... I feel disconnected from everyone?? all my friends are on normal schedules and by the time I'm awake they're already doing dinner plans and I'm just getting ready for work

That social isolation piece is honestly the hardest part of night shift life, and people who haven't done it don't really get it. You're living in a parallel timeline. A couple things that might help: try to protect one or two hours on your days off that overlap with friends' free time, even if it means being a little tired. And see if you can connect with other night shift people at Lurie — they're the only ones who truly understand the schedule. Even just grabbing coffee with a coworker before a shift can help you feel less like you're on a different planet.

I actually do have a couple of nurse friends on the same rotation so maybe we should actually hang out outside of work instead of just complaining in the break room together lol. and yeah my days off I try to see my family but sometimes I just sleep through the whole day because my body doesn't know what's happening

Those break room friendships are gold — definitely try to take them outside the hospital, even if it's just getting breakfast after a shift together. As for your days off, your body is confused because you're probably flipping your schedule back and forth. Sleep experts say night shift workers do better if they stay semi-nocturnal even on days off, but I know that's basically impossible when you want to see family and live a normal life. A compromise is shifting your sleep by a few hours on off days instead of fully flipping — like sleeping 4am to noon instead of trying to do a normal 11pm to 7am.

4am to noon actually sounds doable honestly because then I could still see people in the afternoon. ok I'm going to try the blackout curtain thing and the podcast wind-down and the not-flipping-my-schedule thing. this was actually helpful thank you, I feel like I just needed someone to lay it all out for me because when you're this tired you can't even think about how to fix being tired you know??

That's exactly it — exhaustion makes it impossible to problem-solve your way out of exhaustion. You're doing really important work taking care of those kids, but you've got to take care of yourself too or you'll burn out. Start with the blackout curtains and the podcast swap since those are the easiest wins, and go from there. And hey, be patient with yourself. Eight months of night shift means your body has some adjusting to do. You've got this.

ay dios mio I just realized I've been sitting here talking for like 20 minutes and I need to leave for my shift in an hour and I haven't even eaten yet lol. ok thank you for real, gonna go heat up a frozen burrito and get my life together. night shift waits for no one

Go eat that burrito and crush your shift! And hey, maybe tomorrow morning try one of those changes — even just the podcast instead of the phone scroll. Small steps. Take care of yourself out there tonight.
